
<br>
<center><img src="https://i.imgur.com/AbCCpQO.png" width="700"></center>


_____

# Spatial data and Interactive maps


```{r}
rm(list = ls()) # start fresh

library(sf)
contrib_zipMap=st_read("https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/mapviz/raw/main/contrib_polygons_light.gpkg")
wa_simple <- read_sf('https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/mapviz/raw/main/maps/WA_State_Boundary.geojson')
```

Before building maps, it is always useful to inspect the variables we have available:

```{r}
names(contrib_zipMap)
```


Interactive maps are useful when readers need to zoom in, move around, and explore the map directly in the browser. In this session we use the `leaflet` package, which is one of the most common tools for interactive maps in R.

A useful way to think about `leaflet` is this:

> We create a map object first, and then keep adding layers to it.


# Building a Simple Interactive Map

We begin with the smallest possible leaflet map:

```{r}
library(leaflet)

leafletObject<- leaflet() %>% # create the leaflet object
                  addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") # add the background
leafletObject
```

This creates a blank interactive map with a background tile layer.

Since we already have the `wa_simple` object, we can now add the Washington State polygon directly:


```{r}
leafletObject%>%
  addPolygons(
    data = wa_simple)
```


That works, but the state outline still looks rough. Let us improve it step by step and create a reusable `baseMap`:

```{r}
baseMap<-leafletObject%>%
  addPolygons(
    data = wa_simple,
    fillColor = "white", #polygon(s) fill color
    weight = 0,    # border width
    fillOpacity = 1) # 0 is totally trasparent, 1 full color

baseMap

```
This `baseMap` will serve as the background for the maps we build next.


# A simple choropleth


We begin with one example: Republican contributions in 2012.

### 1. Subset to create `map_Rep2012`:

```{r}
map_Rep2012 <- contrib_zipMap[
  contrib_zipMap$election_year == 2012 &
  contrib_zipMap$party == "REPUBLICAN", ]
```

### 2. Create a color palette

Because `share_contributions_cat` is a categorical variable, we use `colorFactor()`.

```{r, message=FALSE}

paletteFun <- colorFactor(
                  palette = "YlOrRd",
                  domain = map_Rep2012$share_contributions_cat
              )
```

Using the full dataset in `domain` helps keep colors consistent across maps.


### 3. Create hover labels

These labels will appear when the cursor moves over a polygon.

```{r}
popUp_labels <- sprintf(
                  "<strong>%s</strong>",
                  map_Rep2012$ZCTA5CE10
                ) |> lapply(htmltools::HTML)
```

### 4. First draft, adding the polygons from `map_Rep2012`

```{r}
Rep2012 <- baseMap %>%
  addPolygons(
    data = map_Rep2012
  )

Rep2012
```


### 5. Now add the fill color

```{r}
Rep2012 <- baseMap %>%
  addPolygons(
    data = map_Rep2012,
    fillColor = ~paletteFun(share_contributions_cat)
  )

Rep2012
```

Now we can already see the choropleth structure.

### 6. Final Touches

Now we improve appearance and interactivity:

#### a. **Retouching the polygons:**

```{r}
rep2012_map <- baseMap %>%
  addPolygons(
    data = map_Rep2012,
    fillColor = ~paletteFun(share_contributions_cat),
    stroke = FALSE,       # remove polygon borders
    fillOpacity = 0.8,    # slightly transparent fill
    label = popUp_labels, # hover text
    labelOptions = labelOptions(textsize = "15px") # change size of text
  )

rep2012_map

```


**A quick reminder:**

* *color* controls the border
* *fillColor* controls the inside of polygons

#### b. **Adding a legend**

```{r}
rep2012_map %>%
  addLegend(
    data = map_Rep2012,
    values = ~share_contributions_cat,
    pal = paletteFun,
    title = "Contributions",
    position = "bottomright"
  )
```

**Key idea** : Interactive choropleth maps are built layer by layer:

* base tiles
* polygons
* colors
* labels
* legend

# Turning Categories into Interactive Layers

Now we create a map where each contribution category is a separate layer. This lets users switch categories on and off.

We use _Democratic contributions in 2012_.

First, let us inspect the category labels:

```{r}
theLabelsForLevels <- sort(unique(contrib_zipMap$share_contributions_cat))
theLabelsForLevels
```

Let's subset to keep democrats in 2012

```{r}
contribWA2012_demo <- contrib_zipMap[
  contrib_zipMap$election_year == 2012 &
  contrib_zipMap$party == "DEMOCRAT", ]
```


Different than before, we will write the color manually. Let's use a color safe [palette] (https://colorbrewer2.org/). Here I will use the colors of the diverging palette "PuOr" for 4 levels (I will copy and paste them into a vector). Notice I will use _transparency_('fillOpacity') for the most common levels:

```{r}
# the colors
PuOr <- c("#e66101", "#fdb863", "#b2abd2", "#5e3c99")

# the opacity for each
theOpacity <- c(1, 0.5, 0.5, 1)
```


Let's take advantage of the `split()` function:


```{r}
demo_splitted <- split(x = contribWA2012_demo,
                       f = contribWA2012_demo$share_contributions_cat)

```


`demo_splitted` is a list of 4 dataframes, one for each category in **share_contributions_cat**

```{r}
length(demo_splitted)
```

This is useful because we want to add each category as a separate interactive layer.

## A Quick Look at the Parallel Objects

Here is an old-fashioned for loop that helps us inspect the pieces in parallel:

```{r}
for (i in seq_along(demo_splitted)) {
  print(dim(demo_splitted[[i]]))   # dimensions of dataframe i
  print(PuOr[i])                   # color for dataframe i
  print(theOpacity[i])             # opacity for dataframe i
  print(names(demo_splitted)[i])   # category name for dataframe i
}
```

**The important lesson is this:**

> We can move through several related objects at the same time by using the same index `i`.Let's use that to build the FOUR leaflet maps, and plot one on top of the other:

```{r}
demo2012_layered_map <- baseMap

for (i in seq_along(demo_splitted)) {
  demo2012_layered_map <- demo2012_layered_map %>%
    addPolygons(
      data = demo_splitted[[i]],
      stroke = FALSE,
      color = PuOr[i],
      fillOpacity = theOpacity[i],
      group = names(demo_splitted)[i] #"group" will inform the layer name
    )
}

demo2012_layered_map
```
At first glance, the result may not look very different. The crucial step is the `group` argument. That is what allows us to connect each layer to an interactive control.

Now we add the layer control:

```{r}
demo2012_layered_map <- demo2012_layered_map %>%
  addLayersControl(
    overlayGroups = names(demo_splitted),
    options = layersControlOptions(collapsed = FALSE)
  )

demo2012_layered_map
```
Now users can turn contribution categories on and off.

**Key idea**: To make categories interactive in leaflet, we often:

- split the data by category
- add each category as its own group
- connect those groups to addLayersControl()

## Adding a Home Button

When users zoom deeply into a map, it may be hard to return to the original statewide view.

Leaflet does not include a home button by default, but we can add one.

First, we define a small JavaScript function:

```{r}
textFun <- "function(btn, map){map.setView([47.751076, -120.740135], 7)}"
```

This tells the map to return to Washington State at zoom level **7**, center on that specific coordinate.

Now we add the button:

```{r}
demo2012_home_map <- demo2012_layered_map %>%
  addEasyButton(
    easyButton(
      icon = "fa-home",
      title = "Zoom to Washington",
      onClick = JS(textFun)
    )
  )

demo2012_home_map
```

Here, `JS()` lets us pass a small JavaScript function to Leaflet.

**Key idea**: A home button is a simple but very useful addition in exploratory interactive maps.


# Interactive maps of spatial points

```{r}
library(sf)
contriWA_1216_RD_points=st_read("contrib_points.gpkg")

```


**Leaflet** is a good option too for spatial points. But not all strategies are good. 

This strategy will plot every point, but since they are more than 300 000, that might not be a good idea (I will not run this code, but you can try it) : 

```{r, eval=FALSE,warning=FALSE, echo=TRUE}

leaflet(contriWA_1216_RD_points) %>%
    addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") %>%
    addCircleMarkers(stroke = F, radius = 0.5)
```

Leaflet offers this smart alternative:
```{r}
leaflet(contriWA_1216_RD_points) %>%
    addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") %>%
    addCircleMarkers(stroke = F, 
                     clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions())

```

The previous map aggregated all the data and show you the counts by closeness, which changes dynamically as you zoom in or out. Let me split by party for 2016:

* Subsetting

```{r}
sub2016=contriWA_1216_RD_points[contriWA_1216_RD_points$election_year==2016,]
```


* Splitting
```{r}
splitted_2016 <- split(x = sub2016,
                       f = sub2016$party)
names(splitted_2016)
```

* Creating the maps

```{r, warning=FALSE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE}

leafDEMO2016=leaflet(splitted_2016$DEMOCRAT) %>%
    addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") %>%
    addCircleMarkers(clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions())

leafREPU2016=leaflet(splitted_2016$REPUBLICAN) %>%
    addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") %>%
    addCircleMarkers(clusterOptions = markerClusterOptions())

```


## Simulating facets

Above, we just created two maps. Let's see use `leafsync` to "facet" manually:

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, fig.height=8}
library(leafsync)
facet2026=sync(leafDEMO2016,leafREPU2016,ncol = 2)
facet2026
```
This "interactive facet" can be improved if we add the home button! 

```{r, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, fig.width=12}


leafREPU2016<-leafREPU2016%>%
    # adding the button
    addEasyButton(
        easyButton(icon="fa-home", # a symbol
                   title="Zoom to Level 1",
                   onClick=JS(textFun)))


facet2026=sync(leafDEMO2016,leafREPU2016,ncol = 2)
facet2026
```

[Go to MAIN session](https://dacss-visual.github.io/mapviz/)
